speaker: interviewee
text: And the house we lived was completely demolished. There was no house anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait, let's go--

speaker: interviewee
text: So across--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Across from-- from our house there was a Polish family living in there. So I went in to her, and I stayed with her a few days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before this happened, 1943, 1944, you're told to disband. Who gave the order to disband?

speaker: interviewee
text: That came from Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Moscow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now why did they order you to do that, or was the war already over basically for them, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You decided to go back to Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after a while I decided to leave Dubno because there wasn't a safe place there either. The Ukrainians, the Banderovcis, used to come and-- and pick out people and they used to shoot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was the town mostly liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out what happened to your family and to the town? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ironically, it-- it is in the-- a book. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] describes what-- what happened to my town. Because there was a German-- a German engineer working in the area there, where they kept on shooting the people. He-- he-- he heard shooting day after day and hour after hour. So he went-- he went to investigate what happens there. So he described it. He described what happened there. They put-- they put the people on the edge of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ditch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ditch. They told them to undress, and then they shot them, and they fell in-- in the ditch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they did this-- the whole town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you now come back to Dubno, you've been away from any kind of communication the whole war. You've been fighting these-- the Germans and-- and-- and-- and the terrorist activities. What did you think was going on in the world around you? And then when you realize-- you still don't know, in 1944 when you come back to Dubno what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know what happened to the Jews. But we knew that they liquidated the-- the ghetto. There was no people in Dubno anymore, no Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what is your reaction now? You come home. Your home's destroyed. You know your family has all been massacred, along with the rest of the town. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we decided we'll go to-- we'll go to Germany. Maybe it'll be easier to get out from Germany to go to America or some other place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the war's not over yet, right? When you get-- in 1944, the war's not over yet and you come back to Dubno. What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were organizing groups. The Russians themselves were organizing the people that they used to live in Poland get a pass and they-- they supply the trains, and we could go to Poland. So I registered for a pass like that and I went-- I went on one of those trains. And I came, first stop was Berlin, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went from Dubno to Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And when we came to Berlin, there was already some Jewish organizations organized there. They told us what to do. So they-- they send us to a camp by the name of-- Potsdam in Germany, in Berlin. And there we stayed-- they called these camps displaced person camp. You stay there for a couple of years, and they moved us from there to closer to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Western Germany. And they moved us to a camp that was called Eschwege. Eschwege. And then we came to Eschwege. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The United Nation supplied us with food, and we tried to live the best way we could right now. I enrolled in an auto mechanics school in-- in Eschwege. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: First I was a student. And then they needed-- they needed instructors. So I was made an instructor because I was good student. So they appointed me instructor in the auto mechanics school. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is in what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: So the end of the war came and went for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you realized what had happened to European Jewry, what-- what was your reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was very frightful. I was-- I was going to try to get a gun again and go shoot these bastards, the Ukrainians. They were-- they were hand in hand with the Germans. If it-- if it wouldn't be for these Ukrainians, then a lot more people would have-- would have stayed alive. The Ukrainians are the ones that they killed. If there were any Jews in the woods hiding, they-- they spied it out and they came and killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say the Ukrainians were just as much at fault?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If not more than the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, just as much as the Germans. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in the Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a Partisan, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet her?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the first aid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you happen to meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to a town. The name of the town was Kuybyshev. So my-- my colleagues told me there is a house over there, there's a Jewish-- Jewish girl there. And I-- I-- I was with a horse. And I-- I drove over where the house was and I looked at my wife. And I pursued her, and I wouldn't let go anymore. I just pursued her all the time. Wherever she went, I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when you were disbanded in 1944, did she go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she has a-- she had a sister and a brother-in-law and they went back to central Poland. They went back to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I followed them there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was on your mind that you were going to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she feel about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently OK, because she married me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what year were you married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gleiwitz, Germany. Gleiwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so then you went to Eschwege and the other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're married now. And you're an instructor in the auto mechanics field. Did you, like, have an interest to-- to do that kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they said to me, Max, you're-- you're the one that's-- that is elected to do it because you're the one who knows it the best. So I took the challenge. I met the challenge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you became an auto mechanic?

speaker: interviewee
text: An auto mechanic instructor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Germany until you came to America? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eschwege-- we came to Eschwege 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we left 1949 for America. No. No, '47, '48. But we came to America in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: But while you're in Germany you have a little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon. Named him after my wife's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you now are going to come to America. Was it a difficult process to try and get to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My wife's sister was here in-- in Minneapolis, and they made papers for us and they brought us over to Minneapolis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your first day in America like?

speaker: interviewee
text: First day, we stopped in Chicago because the friends that-- that were with us in Eschwege stayed in Chicago and they wanted us to stop there. So we stopped in Chicago, and we stayed with our friends for two, three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of Chicago? Big city. Had you ever seen a big city like that before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I've never seen a big city like that before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so were you impressed with Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much so. It was very dirty city, very dirty city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did you wind up here in Minneapolis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my wife's sister and brother-in-law lived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so a couple days later you had arrived in Minneapolis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Early. No, late '48. Late '48, early '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you got to see the winter in Minneapolis. How'd you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we went through winters like that in Poland. We had snow, a lot of snow also there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so was the language a problem for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the language was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do when you got to Minneapolis, in terms of finding a job or getting settled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was auto mechanic instructor, I went to Ford Motor Company Twin Cities assembly plant. I got hired there. I worked for Ford for 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Assembly work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say assembly work--

speaker: interviewee
text: Assembly work, you put together parts. You have a specific part to put together and assemble it. That's what assembly work is.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, what-- like, in terms-- what did you do? I mean, what part specifically? Did you put, like, a tire onto a car, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: A carburetor onto an engine?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first job was to-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] was to-- electrical wiring. To put electrical wiring. I put it-- I put it in-- in the car and outside of the car where-- where in another area they-- they put the rest of the automobile there. And-- and they put together the electrical stuff that I put in. So that was my job there. Then-- then I took a test and I became an inspector. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inspector, I worked, let's see, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1950. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, no, no. Wait a minute. '49 I came here. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think I worked till '55 as an inspector. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until when? When did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Retired in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you retired from Ford.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-in-law opened a restaurant. I helped him out in the restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? OK, let me get this straight. You came to America when, in 1949?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked for Ford until--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so 1949 to--

speaker: interviewer
text: '79, you worked for Ford.

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 30 years, from '49 through '79.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that time you became an inspector, around 1955 until you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you helped your son-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: In his business, in the restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived through so much and so much has happened to you. Tell me about your belief in-- in Judaism, or what it means to be a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very hard to be a Jew. But if you believe in it, it takes what-- what comes your way and-- and you go on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your beliefs at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My beliefs are there is a God. I believe in the God of Abraham. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually everything will-- will be OK when Messiah will come. When that's going to happen, I don't know. We're waiting for the Messiah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, 2,000 years we wait now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so we'll wait another 1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message would you like to pass on to your children, to your wife, and to other people that may see this tape, about what you've lived through? What message do you have for them? [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No matter how grave the situation is, just [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] believe in God and God will help you out of everything. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Well, maybe we can look at some pictures. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, Max. Tell us about this picture. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That was on the ship, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle of-- of the Atlantic someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, do you remember that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that picture, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I can't remember who took that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, my wife, and my two-year-old son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, Max. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was graduating day from-- from the auto mechanics school in Eschwege. So we went out, all the students and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] instructors, we got together and decided to take a-- a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it take very long to get everybody together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it didn't take very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looks like everybody had to kind of crowd in there. And so where in this picture are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the left side, leaning on my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Left shoulder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? Go in there. And-- and-- and who-- above that fellow that's really looking into the camera there straight on, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was-- he was the director of the school, I think. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy that? Working on cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Great, Max. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was stuffy inside in the-- in the ship, so I wanted to get out to get some fresh air. And a friend of mine took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is on the way to America again, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on the way to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tell me about this picture, Max. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to help my wife feed my little boy and this is after I fed him. I held him up to burp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now is this taken here, or where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Eschwege.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Eschwege, too. Was he a problem, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a problem, huh? In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't want to eat. He didn't want to eat. And young parents, you know, we didn't know what's wrong. But he didn't want to eat, so we always worried about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, Yeah, but he developed OK, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not quite?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he-- he was a problem in other ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but he turned out OK, I guess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he turned out OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you gotta remember, he's going to see this. Good. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is you again, right, Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself and my little girl. She must have been about 2 and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is-- you're leaning up against a-- a Ford.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wouldn't drive a GM, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they would have-- they would have chased me out of the plant if I would have driven a GM.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you remember the conditions with this picture? Or just one summer day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to come home from work and my little girl used to wait for me on the sidewalk. I used to pick her up and throw her around my head. And this time I decided to-- to hold her, to show the car off, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of a Kodak moment, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, tell us about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I think we moved into this house so I wanted to send a picture to my sister in Israel so she could see the house. So we took a picture in front of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in St. Louis Park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] 2845 Sumter Avenue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your first house you ever owned, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretty proud?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was pretty proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: The American dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: American Dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When was this picture taken, Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: '51, and the occasion, let's see. What was the occasion? I think my wife got a new dress and it was a pretty dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you didn't have any pictures of-- of your wedding, did you? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got married in Poland they didn't have any settings up for pictures or stuff like that. We got married in the house where we lived. We invited all our friends and family and we served, after that, dinner, deli stuff. And my sister-in-law baked some sponge cakes, and coffee and vodka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But no cameraman.

speaker: interviewee
text: No cameraman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, because I-- I asked your wife about a wedding picture and she says, there was no cameraman so, couldn't take any pictures. And what's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? Good lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she's a terrific lady. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Great, Max. You told us about a poem that you wanted to recite for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have eight poems. I sent them away to a publisher to be, a book should be published. So far I haven't heard anything yet. I can tell you one poem. This-- this poem I wrote about my own plight.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you write this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I had my stroke, 1989. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is only one. You don't want any more, do you? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: One more?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and tell us a little bit about what this poem is about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next poem, we got a-- a Yiddish club. We meet once a week. And there are different dialects and different-- for instance, there are Polish, Litvaks, and Romanish. They all-- they all pronounce different very things. For instance, a Litvak says--

speaker: interviewer
text: [YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [YIDDISH] . And a Polish said [YIDDISH]. All it is is fish. But they pronounce it different. But when I came in that club to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to explain certain things where they were starting arguing among themselves, so I decided to write a poem about it. And I said, in effect--

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Can you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can explain it. It isn't important how you pronounce it, what's important is how you prepare it. That's the explanation.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a nutshell. Can you explain your first poem that you talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first poem is when I got my stroke. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know what happened to me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I was kind of mixed up, [YIDDISH]. So I decided to write it down so I can have it-- have it whenever I want to refer to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it had to do with, in the-- in the 20th century they could send men to the moon, but they couldn't do anything to cure this terrible--

speaker: interviewee
text: To cure the terrible disease, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other poems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to stop it and maybe Max can think of it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
